card.intuition: Pre-train on a large source task, then fine-tune on a smaller target task. Feature transfer: freeze the backbone and train only the head — fast but less accurate. Full fine-tuning: update all weights — best accuracy but requires careful learning rate scheduling. Gradual unfreezing: unfreeze layers from top to bottom during fine-tuning — balances stability and adaptation.
card.analogy: Learning a second language as a native English speaker: your knowledge of grammar structure (pre-trained features) transfers — you don't learn language from scratch. You only need targeted practice (fine-tuning) on the new language's specific vocabulary and exceptions.
card.time_complexity: Pre-training: O(large data); Fine-tuning: O(small data × epochs)
card.space_complexity: O(model_size) — same as the base model
when_to_use: Small target dataset where training from scratch would overfit
Domain-specific tasks: medical imaging with ImageNet pre-trained backbone
NLP: pre-trained BERT/GPT as backbone for downstream classification
gotchas: Negative transfer: source and target domains too different — low-level features may not help
Use smaller learning rates when fine-tuning (1e-5 to 1e-4) to not destroy pre-trained features
Layer-wise learning rate decay: lr × 0.9^layer_from_top — lower layers get smaller lr

exercise.prompt: Implement layer-wise learning rate decay for fine-tuning.
exercise.starter_code:
def layer_wise_lr(base_lr, n_layers, decay=0.9):
    """
    Compute per-layer learning rates with decay from top to bottom.
    Layer n_layers-1 (output/top) gets base_lr.
    Layer 0 (input/bottom) gets the smallest rate.
    Returns list of learning rates [lr_layer_0, lr_layer_1, ..., lr_layer_n-1].
    """
    lrs = []
    for layer_idx in range(n_layers):
        # Distance from the top layer
        depth_from_top = (n_layers - 1) - layer_idx
        lr = ___________
        lrs.append(lr)
    return lrs
hints: Layer-wise decay: lr = base_lr × decay^(distance_from_top). The top layer gets base_lr × decay^0 = base_lr; bottom layers get exponentially smaller rates.
exercise.test_code:
lrs = layer_wise_lr(base_lr=1e-3, n_layers=4, decay=0.9)
assert len(lrs) == 4
# Top layer (index 3) gets base_lr
assert abs(lrs[3] - 1e-3) < 1e-9
# Layer 2 gets base_lr * 0.9
assert abs(lrs[2] - 1e-3*0.9) < 1e-12
# Layer 1
assert abs(lrs[1] - 1e-3*0.81) < 1e-12
# Layer 0 gets smallest
assert lrs[0] < lrs[1] < lrs[2] < lrs[3]
# decay=1: all same
lrs_flat = layer_wise_lr(1e-3, 4, decay=1.0)
assert all(abs(lr - 1e-3) < 1e-12 for lr in lrs_flat)
print('PASS')
